message.content:
## Adversarial Claim Verifier (voter 2/3)

Be SKEPTICAL. Try to REFUTE this claim. ≥2/3 refutations kill it.

## Research question
DESIGNING AND TRAINING A VOICE / TTS MODEL TO BE MORE COMPREHENSIBLE AT HIGH-SPEED LISTENING. Deliver BOTH the underlying science AND concrete recommendations for a local open-source TTS podcast pipeline (currently uses Kokoro TTS). Cover these 6 angles: (1) ACOUSTIC/PROSODIC PROPERTIES that survive time-compression / make a voice intelligible when sped up — "clear speech"/hyperarticulation, vowel-space expansion, consonant emphasis/sharper consonant-vowel transitions, modulation-spectrum shape, F0/pitch characteristics, and pause/rhythm structure. (2) FORMANT SYNTHESIS vs NEURAL TTS at extreme speed — why blind screen-reader power users overwhelmingly prefer old formant synthesizers (Eloquence, DECtalk, eSpeak-NG) over natural neural voices at 18-22 syllables/second; consistency, crispness, latency, predictability; quantify the naturalness-vs-intelligibility trade-off. (3) RATE-AWARE / CONTROLLABLE TTS GENERATION — conditioning or fine-tuning a neural TTS to NATIVELY generate fast-but-clear speech (speaking-rate control, prosody control, training on clear-speech/Lombard-speech corpora) vs. post-hoc time-scale modification; CRITICAL: how much MEASURABLE comprehensibility gain does native fast generation or fine-tuning actually buy over simply time-compressing a normal voice? (the user will only invest in fine-tuning/training if the gain is significant). (4) DSP ENHANCEMENT for intelligibility at speed — formant/clarity enhancement, spectral shaping + dynamic-range compression, consonant boosting, content-aware PER-PHONEME variable-rate compression (compress steady-state vowels more, preserve consonant transitions), and modulation-envelope enhancement; these apply to ANY engine's output. (5) CONCRETE ENGINES/VOICES & CONFIG KNOBS — head-to-head comparison of which TTS engines/voices are most intelligible at high speed (Kokoro, Piper, eSpeak-NG/Eloquence, RHVoice, Azure/Google neural, ElevenLabs) and the tunable settings (speaking rate, pitch, pause length) that improve speed-comprehensibility. (6) HOW TO MEASURE "comprehensibility at speed" — objective intelligibility metrics (STOI, STMI/spectro-temporal modulation index, modulation-spectrum analysis) and practical subjective A/B / MUSHRA listening-test protocols, so one can decide whether a fine-tune cleared a "significant gain" bar before committing. CONSTRAINTS/CONTEXT: pipeline runs LOCALLY/on-device (open-source Kokoro), so favor approaches compatible with local inference. Output should serve TWO use cases: a normal natural-sounding default voice for general students, AND an optional aggressively speed-optimized track for trained power listeners (robotic is acceptable for that track if measurably clearer). For EACH effort tier (pick/configure a voice < DSP post-processing < fine-tune an open model < train from scratch), state the expected comprehensibility gain, the cost/effort, and whether it is worth it. Prefer peer-reviewed and authoritative sources; include practitioner/community knowledge from the blind/screen-reader speed-listening community where relevant.

## Claim under review
"Latency/responsiveness is a decisive factor: screen reader users rapidly skip and interrupt speech, so the engine must begin producing audio immediately and restart instantly, whereas neural engines like Supertonic and Kitten TTS cannot start until a full text chunk is available."

**Source:** https://github.com/orgs/espeak-ng/discussions/2338 (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Supertonic and Kitten TTS cannot begin generating speech until they have an entire chunk of text."

## Checklist
1. Is the claim actually supported by the quote, or is it an overreach/misread?
2. WebSearch for contradicting evidence — does any credible source dispute or heavily qualify this?
3. Is the source quality sufficient for the claim's strength? (extraordinary claims need primary sources)
4. Is the claim outdated? (check dates — old claims about fast-moving fields are suspect)
5. Is this a marketing claim / press release / cherry-picked benchmark / forum speculation?

**refuted=true** if: unsupported by quote / contradicted / low-quality source for strong claim / outdated / marketing fluff.
**refuted=false** ONLY if: claim is well-supported, current, and source quality matches claim strength.
Default to refuted=true if uncertain.

Structured output only. Evidence MUST be specific.

attachment.content:
- caveman: Ultra-compressed communication mode. Cuts token usage ~75% by speaking like caveman while keeping full technical accuracy. Supports intensity levels: lite, full (default), ultra, wenyan-lite, wenyan-full, wenyan-ultra. Use when user says "caveman mode", "talk like caveman", "use caveman", "less tokens", "be brief", or invokes /caveman. Also auto-triggers when token efficiency is requested.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

# Analysis of Web Page Content

I cannot find any discussion about latency, responsiveness, streaming, skipping/interrupting speech, Supertonic, or Kitten TTS in the provided web page content.

The page shows a GitHub discussion titled "The State of Modern AI Text To Speech Systems for Screen Reader Users #2338" posted by fabiolimace on January 14, 2026. However, the actual discussion content is not displayed in the page excerpt provided. 

The only substantive text is an introductory quote: "The past year has seen an explosion in new text to speech engines based on neural networks, large language models, and machine learning. But has any of this advancement offered anything to those using screen readers?"

The page includes a reference to an external article by Samuel Proulx (published January 5, 2026) at `stuff.interfree.ca`, but the article's full text is not included in this web page content.

To find the requested information about latency, neural engine processing, and screen reader user interruption capabilities, you would need to access the linked article directly or view the complete discussion thread.

Web search results for query: "Supertonic TTS streaming latency first chunk text generation"

Links: [{"title":"Supertonic 3 TTS: Lightning Fast On-Device Text-to-Speech System","url":"https://supertonictts.com/"},{"title":"Open Source Project of the Day (Part 11): Supertonic - Lightning-Fast On-Device Multilingual TTS - DEV Community","url":"https://dev.to/wonderlab/open-source-project-of-the-day-part-11-supertonic-lightning-fast-on-device-multilingual-tts-50hp"},{"title":"SupertonicTTS: Towards Highly Efficient and Streamlined Text-to-Speech System | OpenReview","url":"https://openreview.net/forum?id=urGk2mIJc3"},{"title":"Supertonic TTS: Lightning Fast On-Device Text-to-Speech System","url":"https://supertonic-tts.com/"},{"title":"Real-Time TTS API for Low-Latency Speech Streaming | 2026 Guide","url":"https://www.camb.ai/blog-post/real-time-tts-api-for-low-latency-speech-streaming"},{"title":"CosyVoice 2: Scalable Streaming Speech Synthesis with Large Language   Models","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2412.10117"},{"title":"Latency-Aware TTS Pipeline","url":"https://www.emergentmind.com/topics/latency-aware-text-to-speech-tts-pipeline"},{"title":"STCTS: Generative Semantic Compression for Ultra-Low Bitrate Speech via Explicit Text-Prosody-Timbre Decomposition","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2512.00451"},{"title":"RelayS2S: A Dual-Path Speculative Generation for Real-Time Dialogue","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2603.23346"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found about Supertonic TTS streaming latency and first chunk text generation:

## Streaming Architecture

Supertonic TTS includes streaming processing as part of its latent-to-speech component, enabling it to handle continuous audio generation without processing the entire input at once.

## Latency Performance

Supertonic TTS generates speech in real-time with zero latency, and since all processing happens on-device, there is no network latency. The system is designed to generate speech at speeds up to 167 times faster than real-time on consumer hardware like the M4 Pro.

## Text Generation and Processing

The TTS pipeline operates directly on raw character-level text and employs cross-attention for text-speech alignment, thus eliminating the need for grapheme-to-phoneme (G2P) modules and external aligners. This streamlined approach contributes to its efficiency in processing text for synthesis.

## System Architecture

SupertonicTTS comprises three components: a speech autoencoder for continuous latent representation, a text-to-latent module leveraging flow-matching for text-to-latent mapping, and an utterance-level duration predictor.

The search results don't provide specific metrics for first-chunk latency times, but the overall architecture is optimized for minimal latency through on-device processing and efficient model design.

Web search results for query: "Kitten TTS streaming chunk text latency screen reader"

Links: [{"title":"GitHub - devnen/Kitten-TTS-Server: Self-host the ultra-lightweight Kitten TTS model with this enhanced API server with an intuitive Web UI, large text processing for audiobooks, and GPU acceleration. · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/devnen/Kitten-TTS-Server"},{"title":"Kitten-TTS : Smallest TTS for CPU | by Mehul Gupta | Data Science in Your Pocket | Medium","url":"https://medium.com/data-science-in-your-pocket/kitten-tts-smallest-tts-for-cpu-24f97186ec6d"},{"title":"Super-Tiny AI: Running Kitten TTS v0.8 on Edge Devices","url":"https://www.sitepoint.com/super-tiny-ai-kitten-tts-edge-devices/"},{"title":"GitHub - second-state/kitten_tts_rs: Rust implementation of KittenTTS. CLI and API server. · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/second-state/kitten_tts_rs"},{"title":"The 6 Best On-Device TTS Models for Voice AI","url":"https://getstream.io/blog/best-on-device-tts-models/"},{"title":"Kitten TTS Text to Speech - Free AI TTS Online | TTS.ai","url":"https://tts.ai/text-to-speech/?model=kitten-tts"},{"title":"Kitten TTS: Open-Source Voice Synthesis for Edge Devices - Sesame Disk","url":"https://sesamedisk.com/kitten-tts-open-source-voice-synthesis/"},{"title":"Show HN: Kitten TTS – 25MB CPU-Only, Open-Source TTS Model | Hacker News","url":"https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=44807868"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found about Kitten TTS in relation to streaming, chunking, text latency, and screen reader applications:

## Streaming and Chunking

Kitten TTS supports streaming audio with parameters for text splitting and chunk size (such as `split_text: true` with `chunk_size: 300`). The system intelligently splits long texts into manageable chunks, processes them sequentially, and seamlessly concatenates the audio.

For real-time applications, the server supports streaming with "stream": true and "response_format": "pcm", returning Server-Sent Events with base64-encoded PCM audio chunks, with each sentence sent as a separate audio chunk. The first chunk is split at the earliest clause boundary for fast initial playback.

## Latency

On-device inference achieves latency that drops to the inference time of the model itself (often under 100ms for short utterances). On-device inference means no network round-trips, and according to real-world benchmarks, Kitten TTS delivers prompt voice responses even on Raspberry Pi-class hardware.

## Screen Reader Applications

You can build screen readers without offloading to the cloud, without latency, and without signing cloud service bills. Kitten TTS is suitable for accessibility tools like local screen readers for visually impaired users, where all text-to-speech occurs securely on the user's own device.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

input.prompt: Quote all text about: latency, responsiveness, screen reader users skipping or interrupting speech, restarting speech, and any mention of Supertonic, Kitten TTS, and whether neural engines can or cannot begin generating speech before a full chunk of text is available. Give verbatim quotes.

content.content:
# Text-to-Speech Latency and Screen Reader User Needs

**On responsiveness and interruption:**
"Users of screen readers quickly jump through text and frequently interrupt the screen reader, and thus require the text to speech system to be able to quickly discard and restart speech."

**On Supertonic's speed limitations:**
"Supertonic and Kitten TTS cannot begin generating speech until they have an entire chunk of text."

**On Supertonic's streaming capability:**
"Supertonic is slightly faster, as it can stream result audio as it becomes available, whereas Kitten TTS cannot start speaking until all of the audio for the chunk is fully generated."

**On speed concerns for screen reader use:**
"But for use in a screen reader, a text to speech system needs to begin generating speech as quickly as possible, rather than waiting for an entire phrase or sentence."

**On accuracy issues with modern neural systems:**
"Both models had a tendency to skip words, read numbers incorrectly, chop off short utterances, and ignore prosody hints from text punctuation."

**On Kitten TTS's phonemizer approach:**
"Kitten TTS is slightly better here, as it uses a deterministic phonemizer to determine the correct way to pronounce words, leaving only the generation of the speech itself up to AI."

content.text:
Now I have the actual source text. The article says:

1. "Users of screen readers quickly jump through text and frequently interrupt the screen reader, and thus require the text to speech system to be able to quickly discard and restart speech." — This supports the responsiveness/interruption part of the claim.

2. "Supertonic and Kitten TTS cannot begin generating speech until they have an entire chunk of text." — This is the exact supporting quote.

3. "Supertonic is slightly faster, as it can stream result audio as it becomes available, whereas Kitten TTS cannot start speaking until all of the audio for the chunk is fully generated."

The claim is well-supported by the article. However, let me check the precision of the claim's framing. The claim says neural engines "cannot start until a full text chunk is available." Note nuance: the article distinguishes that Supertonic *can stream result audio* once it has the text chunk, while Kitten cannot even start audio until all audio is generated. The claim conflates two things but the load-bearing assertion (cannot *begin* until a full text chunk is available) is directly quoted. Let me verify whether this is technically accurate / contradicted by other sources, since the earlier searches suggested Supertonic has "streaming."

input.prompt: Does SupertonicTTS use an utterance-level duration predictor? Does it require the entire input text/utterance before generating, or can it start generating from partial text input incrementally? Quote anything about utterance-level processing, duration prediction, and whether it operates on full utterances vs streaming partial text.

input.query: Supertonic TTS utterance-level duration predictor full text required non-streaming input

content.content:
Web search results for query: "Supertonic TTS utterance-level duration predictor full text required non-streaming input"

Links: [{"title":"SupertonicTTS: Towards Highly Efficient and Streamlined Text-to-Speech System","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2503.23108v3"},{"title":"SupertonicTTS: Towards Highly Scalable and Efficient Text-to-Speech System","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2503.23108v1"},{"title":"SupertonicTTS: Towards Highly Efficient and Streamlined Text-to-Speech System | OpenReview","url":"https://openreview.net/forum?id=urGk2mIJc3"},{"title":"[2503.23108] SupertonicTTS: Towards Highly Efficient and Streamlined Text-to-Speech System","url":"https://arxiv.org/abs/2503.23108"},{"title":"[2503.23108v3] SupertonicTTS: Towards Highly Efficient and Streamlined Text-to-Speech System","url":"https://arxiv.org/abs/2503.23108v3"},{"title":"Supertonic 3 TTS: Lightning Fast On-Device Text-to-Speech System","url":"https://supertonictts.com/"},{"title":"Application of ASV for Voice Identification after VC and Duration   Predictor Improvement in TTS Models","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2406.19243"},{"title":"FluidInference/supertonic-3-coreml · Hugging Face","url":"https://huggingface.co/FluidInference/supertonic-3-coreml"},{"title":"Unsupervised TTS Acoustic Modeling for TTS with Conditional Disentangled   Sequential VAE","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2206.02512"},{"title":"Supertone's Supertonic v3: On-Device TTS Tackles Language Barriers and Reading Glitches | The Coders Blog | Home","url":"https://thecodersblog.com/supertones-supertonic-v3-on-device-tts-tackles-language-barriers-and-reading-glitches/"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found about Supertonic TTS and its utterance-level duration predictor:

## Overview

SupertonicTTS is a novel text-to-speech system designed to effectively address the limitations of contemporary TTS models. The system comprises a speech autoencoder, flow-matching-based text-to-latent module, and utterance-level duration predictor.

## Duration Predictor Architecture

SupertonicTTS features an utterance-level duration predictor with a simple, lightweight architecture. An utterance-level text embedding and a reference embedding are obtained using a small number of ConvNext blocks and attention layers, then concatenated and transformed into a scalar value representing the total speech duration via linear layers, resulting in approximately 0.5M parameters.

## Non-Streaming Input Requirements

At inference time, the proposed framework requires the total length of latent representations to be synthesized. Unlike conventional phoneme-level duration models, the duration predictor is optimized to estimate the total speech duration. This means the system needs access to the complete input text upfront to predict the overall utterance duration, making it a non-streaming architecture.

## Key Advantage

SupertonicTTS is expected to be robust to errors in duration estimation, compared to other TTS models that rely on phoneme-level durations.
